title: Ghost State of Light
authors: R. M. de Boer, C. Toebes, Jan Klars, S. R. K. Rodriguez
abstract: We report the observation of a long-lived non-stationary state of light in a single-mode optical cavity. The observed state is a ghost of a saddle-node bifurcation which creates a bottleneck in phase space. While such ghosts are known to exist, accessing them is challenging because it requires a mechanism that steers the relaxation pathway away from the true attractor and into the bottleneck where the ghost emerges. Here we identify such a mechanism, namely a nonlinear response with memory. Our experimental system leverages this mechanism, enabling us to observe ghost states with lifetimes exceeding the cavity photon lifetime by more than ten orders of magnitude, even in the presence of strong fluctuations. The ghost manifests as a plateau in the relaxation dynamics of the cavity transmission, reminiscent of prethermalization. We show how the ghost lifetime depends on the memory time and the distance to the bifurcation, and we observe signatures of scaling in the distribution of ghost lifetimes at fixed driving conditions. Our work establishes minimal conditions for realizing parametrically long-lived non-stationary states.
summary: This paper demonstrates how a nonlinear system with memory can be used to trap light in a 'ghost state' near a bifurcation point. By using the thermal relaxation of olive oil, the authors show that trajectories can be steered into phase-space bottlenecks, creating extremely long-lived intensity plateaus.
main_problem: The challenge of experimentally observing and accessing 'ghost states'—long-lived, non-stationary bottlenecks in phase space that occur near a saddle-node bifurcation.
main_result: The researchers identified that a nonlinear response with memory can steer relaxation pathways into the bottleneck, enabling the observation of ghost states with lifetimes exceeding the cavity photon lifetime by over ten orders of magnitude.
method: The study combines experimental measurements in a microcavity with numerical simulations using the xSPDE toolbox and an overdamped Langevin equation approach.
model_or_system: A plano-concave Fabry-Perot microcavity filled with olive oil, which provides a thermo-optical nonlinearity with a time-delayed (memory) response, driven by a continuous-wave laser.
key_observables: Cavity transmission/intensity, plateau duration (ghost lifetime), and phase-space velocity.
important_parameters: Memory time (tau), laser-cavity detuning (Delta), dissipation rate (Gamma), and nonlinearity strength (U).
main_assumptions: The simulations use a simplified memory time shorter than the experimental value to manage computational costs, and the 1D effective potential approximation assumes dynamics are primarily along the nullcline.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows experimental and simulated transmission plateaus; Fig 2 displays scaling laws for plateau duration; Fig 3 illustrates relaxation trajectories in the complex plane; Fig 6 provides a parameter space map of the bifurcation.
paper_structure: Introduction to ghost states and the bottleneck problem, description of the experimental microcavity setup, presentation of the stochastic model and simulations, analysis of scaling laws and memory effects, and discussion of the implications for non-stationary states.
why_interesting: It provides a way to experimentally access and control long-lived transient states in non-equilibrium systems using memory-induced steering, which is highly relevant to open quantum systems and dissipative phase transitions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Experimental input (top, green curve) and transmitted (bottom) power of our optical cavity. Blue and gray curves correspond to a laser-cavity detuning close to and far away from the saddle-node bifurcation (∆b), respectively. The inset in (a) is an illustration of the optical cavity under study. (b) Simulations corresponding to the experiments in (a). The green curve is the amplitude A of the driving laser. Orange and blue curves are the intracavity intensity in the absence and presence of noise, respectively. The black curve corresponds to a cavity without memory (τ →0), in equal proximity to the saddle-node bifurcation as the orange and blue curves. Only in the presence of...

FIG. 2. (a) Plateau duration as a function of distance from the bifurcation for different memory times τ. Dashed black lines are inverse square-root fits to the data. (b) Plateau duration as a function of τΓ at (∆−∆b)/Γ = 6 · 10−7.

FIG. 3. (a) Relaxation trajectories in the complex α plane for the cavity with memory as a colored curve (with color representing instantaneous velocity) and for the memoryless cavity in black. The gray arrows represent the vector flow of the force ⃗F in Eq. (1) with τ →0. (b) Relaxation trajectory for the cavity with memory in the three dimensional phase space of αI, αR, and w.

FIG. 4. Effective phase-space potentials at the start and end of our step-like protocol, plotted in gray and black, respec- tively. The flat region in the black potential is the bottleneck where the ghost state emerges.

FIG. 5. Distribution of ghost lifetimes obtained from 1000 experimental trajectories. The red line is a power-law with exponent -3/2, which is the expected value (see supplemental material [35]) in the absence of a stochastic initial condition in the detuning ∆.
